title: Psalm 103: Part 2
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro

outline.point: I. The Lord Has Shown You That He Cares (Psalm 103:6-7)
subpoints: A. The Exodus is the great proof of God's care — the type of redemption from sin
B. The oppressed are God's people enslaved to sin, not primarily a social justice text
C. God's revealing Himself to us is itself an act of deliverance — He was not bound to do so

outline.point: II. The Lord Shows Mercy Because He Is Merciful (Psalm 103:8-10)
subpoints: A. Psalm 103:8 is a word-for-word quote from Exodus 34 — God's self-revelation to Moses
B. God abounds in mercy — it cannot be contained or exhausted
C. Even God's temporary displeasure with His people does not change their eternal standing

outline.point: III. God Has Not Dealt with Us According to Our Sins (Psalm 103:10)
subpoints: A. Whatever you are going through, you are a lot better than you deserve
B. God dealt with His Son according to your sins so He need not deal with you that way
C. Paul's 'light and momentary affliction' — even his suffering was nothing compared to what he deserved

outline.point: IV. The Greatness and Mechanics of God's Forgiveness (Psalm 103:11-14)
subpoints: A. Mercy as high as the heavens — you can never exhaust God's grace
B. Transgressions removed as far as east is from west — the guilt is truly gone
C. Do not add penance — Christ's righteousness imputed to us is sufficient

outline.point: V. Forgiveness Must Shape Our Relationships
subpoints: A. God nurses no grudge; we love to keep quarrels going — the opposite of how God treats us
B. Bitterness is contrary to who God is — we are not victims but perpetrators
C. Can you truthfully pray: 'Forgive us as we forgive others'?

summary:
The second in a four-part series on Psalm 103, this sermon examines verses 6-14 and focuses on God's covenant mercy as the driving force behind His relationship with His people. Pastor Lyro begins with the Exodus as the great biblical portrait of what it means to be saved — God executing righteousness and justice by delivering His people from slavery to sin — and notes that God's revealing Himself to us in Scripture is itself an act of undeserved grace. The sermon then unpacks Psalm 103:8, a direct quote from Exodus 34, to show that God does not merely have some mercy; He abounds in it, and that abundance should drive us to prayer rather than away from it in fear.

The heart of the sermon is verse 10: God has not dealt with us according to our sins, because He dealt with His Son according to our sins. Drawing on Paul in 2 Corinthians 4 and Romans 8, Pastor Lyro argues that whatever trial any believer faces is incomparably light when set against the eternal weight of glory — and that our current sufferings are far less than what we actually deserve. The sermon then turns pastoral and personal: if we truly grasped the magnitude of God's forgiveness, there would be no room for bitterness in our hearts toward those who have wronged us. The session closes by challenging the congregation to honestly examine whether they can pray the sixth petition of the Lord's Prayer — 'forgive us our debts as we forgive our debtors' — and calls them to forgive as those who have been forgiven at the great cost of God's own Son.
transcript:
## Introduction: Review of Part One

I said it two weeks ago and I will say it again: there are no adequate pictures of God in this world. People have tried to create images that represent God, but nothing does any justice to all the glory and all the attributes of God. The only place we find an accurate portrait of God is in his Word. And Psalm 103 gives us a very beautiful portrait of our God — a God who is merciful, a God who is compassionate. Whatever Psalm I am reading at any given moment seems to be my favorite, and Psalm 103 is a glorious Psalm that paints the picture of a great God.

Two weeks ago, in verses 1-5, David told us who God is by describing the benefits that flow from God onto his people — all these benefits related to who he is. We saw that God is a God who has entered into covenant with his people to love, redeem, and sanctify them through the forgiveness of their sins. He heals all their diseases. He redeems their life from the pit. He crowns his people with loving-kindness and tender mercies. He satisfies them with good things. We need to keep reminding ourselves of God's character, because all these benefits flow from God's nature. Brothers and sisters, God cannot help but forgive, redeem, crown, and satisfy his people who belong to him through Jesus Christ.

In today's section — verses 6-14 — David describes God's relationship with us, framing it in terms of God's forgiveness, because he is merciful and compassionate. What drives God's relationship with us is forgiveness and reconciliation. I hope to show throughout this sermon that the same thing should drive our relationships with one another — forgiveness and reconciliation — because that is what our God does for us, and we must be willing to do the same for others.

## I. The Lord Has Shown You He Cares (Psalm 103:6-7)

> *"The Lord executes righteousness and justice for all who are oppressed. He made known His ways to Moses, His acts to the children of Israel."* (Psalm 103:6-7)

The proof that God gives that he cares for you is the Exodus. David uses the Exodus to illustrate God's care. The Exodus is the great redemptive act of God in the Old Testament. If you want to know what it means to be saved, you look at the Exodus. If you want to know what God is doing for his people, you look at the Exodus. It is the illustration and type of God's redemption.

When verse 6 speaks of "the oppressed," the reference is to God's people in slavery in Egypt — the ultimate type of slavery to sin. David here is not primarily concerned with issues of social justice or freeing people from social oppression and poverty. When people interpret this verse that way, they are not accounting for the biblical imagery used throughout Scripture. The interpretation that David is calling us to greater awareness of social injustice does not find its origin in the Word of God but in the writings of critical theorists with Marxist or Hegelian ideological influences. Brothers and sisters, the greatest oppression you and I face is the oppression of sin — and God delivers us from that.

Notice in verse 7 that the execution of righteousness and justice is demonstrated in God's *making himself known* to us. God's revealing himself to us is itself an act of deliverance. God was not bound to show himself to you. He did not have to do that. You would still owe him allegiance even if you could not know him. Yet he reveals himself to you.

Our confession, in the chapter on the covenants, says: "The distance between God and the creature is so great that although reasonable creatures do owe obedience to him as their Creator, yet they could never have any fruition of him as their blessedness and reward but by some voluntary condescension on God's part, which he hath been pleased to express by way of covenant."

And even the 40 years the Israelites spent wandering in the wilderness were part of God's deliverance of his people. We sometimes think deliverance equals only what we think is good. Yet even 40 years in the desert were part of God's deliverance. In Deuteronomy 8:2-3, as the people stood at the edge of the Jordan ready to enter the Promised Land, Moses reminded them:

> *"And you shall remember that the Lord your God led you all the way these forty years in the wilderness, to humble you and test you, to know what was in your heart, whether you would keep His commandments or not. So He humbled you, allowed you to hunger, and fed you with manna which you did not know nor did your fathers know, that He might make you know that man shall not live by bread alone; but man lives by every word that proceeds from the mouth of the Lord."* (Deuteronomy 8:2-3)

The Lord has shown his mercy in delivering you from sin — and even through struggles, he continues to deliver you.

## II. The Lord Is Merciful Because He Is Merciful (Psalm 103:8-10)

The second thing I want us to see is that the Lord shows mercy to you *because he is merciful*. That may sound obvious, but I do not think we think about it very much. We tend to think more about what God does than about what God is. The reason he is merciful to you is because being merciful is part of his nature.

> *"The Lord is merciful and gracious, slow to anger, and abounding in mercy."* (Psalm 103:8)

This is a word-for-word quotation from Exodus 34, where God revealed himself to Moses in those very terms. Even though we are quick to rebel against God, God is slow to anger because he is merciful and gracious. Notice that God is not *a little bit* merciful and *a little bit* gracious. As Elder Nick Anderson was pointing out in Sunday school this morning — we sometimes think that Jesus is frowning on us, that God is watching every little slip and waiting to exhaust his patience with us. But verse 8 says he *abounds* in mercy. That is how God reveals himself to you: as a God who abounds in mercy — overflowing, nothing can contain it. There is no container large enough for all of God's mercy.

That led the prophet Micah, writing a few hundred years after David, to say:

> *"Who is a God like You, pardoning iniquity and passing over the transgression of the remnant of His heritage? He does not retain His anger forever, because He delights in mercy. He will again have compassion on us, and will subdue our iniquities. You will cast all our sins into the depths of the sea."* (Micah 7:18-19)

Why does God do that? Because that is who he is. God abounds in steadfast love and covenant mercy because of who he is.

Consider the cry of even the poorest debtor in Exodus 22. There is a series of case laws there about collecting debts, and one law says that if you take a poor person's outer cloak as security for a debt, you must return it at nightfall because that cloak may be the only thing keeping them warm through the night. Exodus 22:27 concludes that discussion: "For that is his only covering, it is his garment for his skin. What will he sleep in? And it will be that when he cries to Me, I will hear, for I am gracious."

Did you hear what God says? If you are not merciful to the poorest debtor and he cries to me, God says, "I am gracious — I will listen to him." Brothers and sisters, we owe far more than a garment's worth of debt. We owe an infinite debt to God because of our sins. But when we cry out to him, he hears us and forgives us, because he is gracious even to the greatest debtor.

Do you cry out to the Lord, knowing he is going to listen? Do you come and say, "Lord, I am a sinner. I need your grace"? He loves that prayer. He answers it every time.

## III. His Anger Is Temporary; His Forgiveness Is Complete (Psalm 103:9-12)

It is true that in verse 9 David acknowledges God will at times discipline and be displeased with us, like a father with a disobeying child:

> *"He will not always strive with us, nor will He keep His anger forever."* (Psalm 103:9)

But even this anger, this displeasure, is temporary. If you are a father who loves your children, you will not allow anything to permanently sever that relationship. But there are times when a child disobeys and you are less than pleased. That is the picture the Spirit gives us here. At times we damage our relationship with God — not God, but the relationship — and there is a period of discipline. But it is temporary. It does not change our eternal status as children of God. There is nothing that can separate you from the love of God in Jesus Christ. David said in Psalm 30:5:

> *"For His anger is but for a moment, His favor is for a lifetime; weeping may endure for a night, but joy comes in the morning."* (Psalm 30:5)

The wilderness wandering is an example of this temporary displeasure. God was displeased with his people. But eventually he brought them into the Promised Land.

As a believer in Jesus Christ, no matter what you are suffering right now, whatever is going on that is negative or contrary in your life, God has not in any way dealt with you according to what your sins deserve. You know how sometimes when someone asks how you are doing, you say: "Better than I deserve"? That is one hundred percent true, one hundred percent of the time, no matter what you are going through.

Look at verse 10:

> *"He has not dealt with us according to our sins, nor punished us according to our iniquities."* (Psalm 103:10)

The reason you are not bearing the eternal consequences of your sin is that God dealt with his *Son* according to your sins. He dealt with his Son according to your sins so that he would not have to deal with you according to your sins. It is the just for the unjust. As 1 Peter 3:18 says:

> *"For Christ also suffered once for sins, the just for the unjust, that He might bring us to God, being put to death in the flesh but made alive by the Spirit."* (1 Peter 3:18)

The unjust is not some terrible person out there somewhere. The unjust is you. It is me.

Jeremiah understood this. When he saw Jerusalem utterly razed to the ground and the temple gone — which is what prompted the book of Lamentations — for the first two chapters and into the third, he has a tone of deep lamentation. But then it clicks: even this is not what they deserved. They deserved far greater destruction. And so in Lamentations 3:22-23:

> *"Through the Lord's mercies we are not consumed, because His compassions fail not. They are new every morning; great is Your faithfulness."* (Lamentations 3:22-23)

Brothers and sisters, whatever you are going through right now or have gone through in the past — it does not come close to what you deserve. You and I deserve eternity in hell, bearing the wrath of God for our sins against a holy and infinite God. And yet we are promised eternity in his presence, experiencing his loving compassion forever.

This truth and the hope of eternal glory kept the Apostle Paul going. In 2 Corinthians 4:16-18:

> *"Therefore we do not lose heart. Even though our outward man is perishing, yet the inward man is being renewed day by day. For our light and momentary troubles are achieving for us an eternal glory that far outweighs them all. So we fix our eyes not on what is seen, but on what is unseen, since what is seen is temporary, but what is unseen is eternal."* (2 Corinthians 4:16-18)

"Our *light* affliction"? By the time Paul wrote that, he had already suffered three shipwrecks — with a fourth still to come. He had been beaten multiple times, receiving the 39 lashes on several occasions. He had been stoned and left for dead. He had been imprisoned, hungry, cold, and betrayed by his own people. And he calls that a light and momentary affliction compared to the glory to come.

I wonder if our discouragement, our doubts, our despair come from not living as pilgrims in this land — not realizing this life is momentary and not all there is. I wonder if our lives would be more filled with joy if we did not treat this present existence as the most important time. If you think this life is the thing that matters most — if you are not craving heaven — I would encourage you to examine your heart and consider the role Jesus actually plays there. I wonder if our lives would be a better display of God's glory if we were actually looking forward to glory.

Are you looking forward to glory? Are you holding the things of this life with open hands, knowing that this present moment is nothing compared to the life to come?

### Forgiveness Flows from What God Is

There is more in verse 9. God will not always strive with us; he will not keep his anger forever. Have you ever noticed that we are often the anti-God on this issue? We love to keep quarrels going and nurse our grievances against others — the exact opposite of what God does with us. We hold on to wrongs, real or perceived. But God, who is infinitely wronged by us, rejoices in forgiving — and he does that at great cost to himself. The cost was his Son.

A few years ago, a well-known deconstruction coach — the word "coach" is a misnomer because they claim there is no right or wrong way to deconstruct, but if there is a coach, someone must be showing you how — said that Jesus only had to pay the price of a weekend, since he died on Friday and rose on Sunday. Brothers and sisters, to think that way is to deeply misunderstand what was happening. On that Friday afternoon, Jesus Christ bore the infinite weight of God's wrath for you on the cross. He gave up his fellowship with his Father to save you. Not because he deserved that — but because you and I deserved exactly that crucifixion, that separation, that bearing of wrath.

I wonder: if we truly understood the magnitude of God's forgiveness, would there be any bitterness left in our hearts? We would be ready to forgive anyone who wronged us. I want to invite you right now to think in your minds about the broken relationships in your life. What have you done to pursue biblical reconciliation? Is there bitterness in your heart toward anyone? Bitterness is contrary to who God is.

We must be people ready to forgive because we have been forgiven much. If we had any idea of the magnitude of God's forgiveness toward us, we would know that we are not the victims — we are the perpetrators of great sin against God. No matter what somebody does to us, what we have done against an infinite, holy God is greater. If we grasped the gospel again, we would courageously pray the sixth petition of the Lord's Prayer: *"Forgive us our debts as we forgive our debtors."* Can you truthfully pray that prayer? Can you come before God and say: "Use the same standard to forgive me that I use to forgive my spouse, my children, my parents, my friends"? Not if you are holding bitterness in your heart. You cannot pray that prayer.

The Lord forgives us at the great cost of himself. And David repeats that in verses 11-14.

## IV. The Mechanics of Forgiveness (Psalm 103:11-14)

Notice that verses 11-14 describe what happens to those who *fear* the Lord. To fear God in the Old Testament is to believe in God. We could accurately substitute "faith" for "fear" here, and the meaning would remain the same. It is the kind of faith James describes — faith that shows itself in obedience.

### God's Mercy Cannot Be Exhausted (v. 11)

> *"For as the heavens are high above the earth, so great is His mercy toward those who fear Him."* (Psalm 103:11)

The Psalmist is not looking at the horizon where the sky appears to meet the earth. He is looking *up* — trying to reach heaven — and knowing he never will. That is how great God's mercy is toward us. Earth and sky never touch when you look upward. Sometimes when we come under great conviction of sin, we feel as if we have used up all of God's grace. The reality: his mercies are new every morning. You can never exhaust God's grace. You can never exhaust God's mercy. This should drive us to prayer — recognizing how much we need it, and how abundantly God supplies it.

### Our Sins Are Removed Beyond Recovery (v. 12)

> *"As far as the east is from the west, so far has He removed our transgressions from us."* (Psalm 103:12)

Take a compass and head east. When will you reach west? Never. David probably did not know about magnetic north, but the Holy Spirit did. And notice he says east and west, not north and south — because if you go north or south, the instruments eventually get confused and you might think you have arrived. But east and west — you never get there. That is how far God has removed the penalty and guilt of your sins from you.

No matter how hard you try to find the penalty for your sin as a believer, you will not find it. It is hidden in Christ. You will never find it again.

And yet we often embrace penance as if God needs it to forgive us, or as if we are earning his favor. Brothers and sisters, the guilt and penalty of your sins have been removed as far as east is from west. You do not need to keep suffering as if to pay for your sins. Sometimes people think that being discouraged, punishing themselves, or feeling down will make God love them more. But when you do that, you are actually being profoundly proud — because you are saying that what Christ the Son of God did for you is not sufficient; that you have to add to it. God has already accepted his Son. Stop trying to atone for your own sins. Just live for his glory — recklessly for his glory.

Our sins were imputed to Christ. His righteousness was imputed to us, declared to be ours. As 2 Corinthians 5:21 says:

> *"For He made Him who knew no sin to be sin for us, that we might become the righteousness of God in Him."* (2 Corinthians 5:21)

The hymn writers had it right: "Nothing in my hands I bring, simply to the cross I cling." And: "You alone, O Christ." Nothing else.

The Holy Spirit wants us to know the immeasurable love of God in the Son. Paul prays in Ephesians 3:

> *"...that you may be able to comprehend with all the saints what is the width and length and depth and height — to know the love of Christ which passes knowledge; that you may be filled with all the fullness of God."* (Ephesians 3:18-19)

As you go deeper and deeper into the love of God, the love of Christ, you are filled with the fullness of God.

### God Is Our Father (v. 13)

This merciful God who forgives us through Jesus Christ is our Father:

> *"As a father pities his children, so the Lord pities those who fear Him."* (Psalm 103:13)

He is the perfect Father who loves us perfectly. He has compassion on those who believe in him. In Isaiah 49:15, the Lord says:

> *"Can a woman forget her nursing child, and not have compassion on the son of her womb? Surely they may forget, yet I will not forget you."* (Isaiah 49:15)

Can you imagine a mother forsaking her nursing child? The closest relationship we can think of — and yet even that can break down. But God says: even when that might happen, I will not forget you. Even when the closest relationships on earth may break down, God will not forsake you.

Our Father does not deny any good thing to us. Are you convinced of that? If you are, then you can ask all things of the Lord, knowing that if we as sinful parents know how to give good things to our children, how much more will our Father in heaven do so? You can cling to Psalm 84:11:

> *"For the Lord God is a sun and shield; the Lord will give grace and glory; no good thing will He withhold from those who walk uprightly."* (Psalm 84:11)

And you might say: "But my children are not saved. My circumstances are painful. I do not have money. I am sick. Look at all the good things God is withholding from me." No — if he has not given you those things, it is because they are not good *for you*, not right now. What you need is Christ and more of him. Not better circumstances — Christ. And this merciful God knows what you need.

### God Knows Our Frame (v. 14)

> *"For He knows our frame; He remembers that we are dust."* (Psalm 103:14)

When we are younger, we think we can conquer the world. As we get older — and perhaps as illness comes — the comparison to dust that falls apart becomes more and more real. That is what we are. From dust we came; to dust we will return. And God knows that. He knows us better than we know ourselves. So whatever he tells us to do, we can do by his grace and Spirit, because he knows who we are. He does not ask us to do anything for which he does not also provide the grace and strength in Jesus Christ.

That is why the best way to know ourselves is not by looking inward at our hearts, or by talking to a therapist who does not know the Scriptures. The best way to know ourselves is by looking into the Scriptures — the royal law of liberty, the mirror of God's Word.

## Conclusion

Brothers and sisters, our God truly is awesome. I do not use that word lightly. He is merciful and gracious. He is slow to anger. He abounds in mercy. And because of who he is, he forgives those who trust in Jesus Christ. That mercy cannot be exhausted. This compassionate God is our Father — a Father who will never leave us nor forsake us. You know that to be true because there is an empty tomb. Christ was reunited with his Father, and in him we who believe are forever the children of the Heavenly Father. Forever. Nothing can separate you from the love of your God in Christ Jesus."
